Title: The northern economic arc: A strategic imperative for Malaysia's future
Article:
WHILE Malaysia's west coast has long driven the nation's growth, the northern and east coast states have quietly waited for their chance to shine.
Rich in resources and strategic location, Kedah, Kelantan, Terengganu and Perlis hold enormous, untapped potential.
Greater economic cooperation among these states could generate benefits that reach far beyond the region, boosting national economic resilience, food security, and regional balance.
One key initiative is the creation of an integrated agriculture belt.
With Kedah and Perlis focusing on rice production, and Kelantan and Terengganu leading fisheries, livestock, and fruit production, the region could become a powerhouse of food supply.
But this is about more than just output - it is about value. Shared food processing and halal certification hubs would enhance agricultural productivity.
Together, these efforts would strengthen Malaysia's food security, reduce dependence on imports, and stabilise prices in major urban areas.
Cross-border trade with Thailand offers another major opportunity. Kelantan and Perlis are not peripheral - they serve as gateways to southern Thailand.
By expanding border trade, improving logistics, and establishing wholesale markets linked to Thailand, the four states could create stronger trade corridors.
From a national perspective, this deepens Malaysia's integration with regional markets and expands opportunities for agricultural and manufacturing exports.
Tourism can also be a catalyst for growth. A four-state tourism circuit - from the beaches of Terengganu, the cultural heritage of Kelantan, island tourism in Langkawi, Kedah, to agro-tourism in Perlis - would offer a diverse and complementary travel experience.
Tourism is currently concentrated around Kuala Lumpur and the west coast, but a northern corridor could distribute income more evenly across Malaysia while attracting longer stays from overseas visitors.
The fisheries and marine economy provides further opportunities for collaboration.
With Terengganu and Kelantan leading deep-sea fisheries, supported by shared processing and cold storage facilities, the seafood sector could be scaled up substantially.
This is more than a regional industry - it is a national asset. In turn, this would reinforce Malaysia's domestic seafood supply while supporting export growth, particularly to Asia and the Middle East.
Malaysia could learn from global leaders like Norway and Denmark, whose Scandinavian model combines sustainable fishing practices, strict quality standards, modern processing, and efficient cold-chain logistics.
The development of a Northern Halal Corridor could also elevate Malaysia's global halal position.
By integrating food processing, cosmetics, and halal pharmaceutical production across the four states, the region would advance Malaysia's ambition to be a global halal hub.
Coordinated branding and export strategies targeting the Middle East and Asean markets could further strengthen international trade.
Improved logistics and transport connectivity is essential for success. Upgrading highways, integrating ports and connecting railway infrastructure such as the East Coast Rail Link would transform the movement of goods and services.
At the national level, enhanced connectivity would boost supply chain efficiency and support the distribution of agricultural and industrial products.
The SME and cottage industry networks can also play a pivotal role. The four states boast strong traditions in crafts, batik and local food industries.
Through shared digital platforms and coordinated promotion, smaller companies could access wider international markets.
Businesses in the East Coast could be further empowered, complementing broader efforts to position SMEs as a central driver of economic growth.
Cooperation in renewable energy and natural resources brings additional national benefits.
Solar and biomass projects in agricultural areas, along with coastal renewable energy initiatives in Terengganu, would contribute to Malaysia's long-term energy diversification while supporting environmentally sustainable development.
Taken together, collaboration among the four states would not only strengthen regional development but also advance Malaysia's broader economic objectives.
By integrating agriculture, trade, tourism, and logistics, Kedah, Kelantan, Terengganu and Perlis could form a complementary growth corridor, enhancing national resilience and ensuring long-term sustainability.
This is not just a regional plan - it is a strategic imperative Malaysia can no longer afford to ignore.
* The writer holds an MBA from the University of Strathclyde in the UK, awarded through the prestigious British Chevening Scholarship. With extensive background in the financial markets and robust background in management education, he also served a prominent think-tank. The opinion expressed in the article are how own and not necessarily represent the views of Business Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EV sales yet to see meaningful surge despite rising fuel prices
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Rising fuel costs driven by geopolitical tensions are beginning to nudge interest in electric vehicles (EVs), but the shift remains gradual, with no meaningful pickup in sales so far.
Market observers told Business Times that while higher petrol prices are raising awareness, they have yet to translate into a clear surge in EV showroom traffic.
Instead, early growth in the local EV segment this year appears to be driven more by pricing and product factors than fuel dynamics.
Electronikar editor and analyst Shamsul Yunos said the increase in EV registrations in the first two months of 2026 was largely supported by the introduction of Proton's e.MAS 5.
"Its aggressive entry price (starting from RM59,800) finally provides an affordable 'on-ramp' for those who were already considering a switch but were waiting for a local, value-driven option.
"The 'oil effect' is there, but the 'Proton effect' is currently the bigger driver of those registration numbers," he said.
The Road Transport Department data showed 3,635 EVs were registered in February, up from 2,160 units in the same month last year.
Industry expert Hezeri Samsuri said interest in EVs is picking up as Malaysians become more aware of the country's reliance on imported fuel.
Even if fuel prices ease following developments in Iran, consumers will remain conscious that prices can rise again, he said.
He added the growing number of EV registrations highlights the need for the government to establish a dedicated agency to oversee the development of public charging infrastructure.
"Right now, our charging point operators have very few guidelines, and there is no proper national-level planning," Hezeri said.
PRICING STILL KEY DRIVER
Dell Zhafry Fuad, a BYD sales advisor, said interest in EVs has been building even before the recent rise in fuel prices, resulting in increased bookings for BYD models.
The BYD Sealion 7 and BYD Atto 3 are among the most popular models, with their competitive pricing and distinctive designs driving appeal, he added.
"Demand for EVs such as the BYD Sealion 7 and Atto 3 has been strong, supported by attractive promotions. The challenge now is ensuring sufficient supply to meet customer demand," he said.
Meanwhile, Shamsul said consumers remain cautious, despite growing interest.
While the reduction in the Budi95 subsidy and rising unsubsidised fuel prices are beginning to have an impact, buyers are still weighing concerns over charging infrastructure and upfront costs.
"If Brent crude stays above US$120 per barrel for more than a quarter, the government may have to adjust subsidised prices further.
"However, we should not overlook plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs). In the short term, they may be a more significant contributor than pure battery electric vehicles.
"Many Malaysians are still cautious about going fully electric due to inconsistent charging infrastructure. A PHEV offers that safety net while allowing them to hedge against rising fuel costs," he said.
SECONDARY MARKET STILL THIN
In the used segment, Carsome group chief business officer Aaron Kee said the EV secondary market remains at an early stage, with limited transaction activity.
Although the platform has seen a noticeable rise in enquiries and search interest, actual transaction volumes remain low, mainly due to limited supply.
"Meaningful new EV volumes only started entering the market from 2023 onwards, so the secondary market has not had enough time to build up inventory. Most EV owners are still within their first ownership cycle," he said.
Kee said that given the limited supply, it is still too early to identify definitive bestsellers, although models such as the BYD Atto 3 and Tesla Model 3 continue to see strong interest.
"These were among the earlier EVs to achieve meaningful volume in Malaysia, so they naturally form the initial pool of used inventory.
"The Tesla Model Y is also gaining steady traction. On the newer end, the Proton e.MAS 7 has generated significant interest.
"While it is still too early for it to appear meaningfully in the used market, its positioning within the mass market suggests it could become a key contributor to used EV supply over time as ownership cycles mature," he added.
Kee said most buyers in the used EV space are first-time EV adopters, typically younger and more digitally savvy.
These buyers are aware of the long-term savings but remain cautious about upfront costs, making the used segment more attractive.
"We are also seeing some households considering a used EV as a second vehicle for daily commuting, while retaining an ICE vehicle for longer journeys where charging infrastructure remains a consideration," he said.
Kee said the main challenge at this stage is building trust, as buyers seek greater assurance on battery condition, ownership history and aftersales support.
As the segment grows, he said platforms will need to strengthen inspection standards and transparency, particularly for EV-specific components.
STRUCTURAL SHIFT NEEDED
Shamsul said the pace of EV adoption will depend on structural changes. Survey data shows that those who switch to EVs rarely return to fully internal combustion vehicles, and if they do, it is typically to hybrids.
He added that achieving mass adoption in Malaysia will require stable and clear government policies on fuel subsidies, enabling consumers to better plan long-term transportation costs.
It will also depend on visible and reliable charging infrastructure, as well as a shift towards local assembly and supply chain development to keep EVs affordable once tax incentives expire.
The secondary market will also play a role, as used EVs with strong value propositions become an attractive entry point for more cautious buyers, Shamsul said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sri Lanka raises electricity costs by up to 40 per cent
Article:
COLOMBO: Sri Lanka announced on Monday a nearly 40 per cent increase in electricity prices from Wednesday as it battles an energy shortage caused by the war in the Middle East.
The Public Utilities Commission of Sri Lanka said further increases were likely in April, in line with global energy prices.
"The increases we announce today were calculated on the basis of prices that prevailed before the Middle East conflict," PUCSL chairman K. P. L. Chandralal told reporters in Colombo.
For most middle-class consumers, a kilowatt hour of electricity will cost 84 rupees (US$0.28), up from 61 rupees, a 39.34 per cent increase, while the lowest consumers – those who use less than 30 kilowatt hours a month – will see their bills rise by 11.11 per cent.
Sri Lanka has raised fuel prices three times this month, increasing them by more than a third, and has announced a four-day working week in a bid to save energy.
The government has also asked the private sector to reintroduce work-from-home arrangements where possible.
The Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway through which some 20 per cent of global crude and gas exports pass in peacetime, has been effectively closed by Iran after US-Israeli strikes on the country late last month sparked a larger regional conflict.
Sri Lanka imports all of its oil and also buys coal for electricity generation. It buys refined petroleum products from India, Singapore, Malaysia and South Korea, while crude oil for its Iran-built refinery is sourced from the Middle East.
The government has warned that the fighting in the Middle East, and any prolonged war, could seriously undermine its efforts to emerge from the economic meltdown of 2022.
Sri Lanka defaulted on its US$46 billion foreign debt in 2022 after the country ran out of foreign exchange. Since then, Colombo has secured a US$2.9 billion IMF bailout.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump threatens to destroy Iran oil island despite claims of talks
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump threatened Monday to destroy Iran's key oil export hub of Kharg Island along with power and desalination plants unless Tehran quickly accepted a deal, even as he suggested that diplomacy was making headway.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, Trump's partner in attacking Iran, said more than half of his military aims had been achieved, even as both leaders refused to put a timeline on the operation that has ignited a regional war and sent global oil prices soaring.
More than one month into the war, Trump said the United States was speaking to a "more reasonable regime" in Tehran, which has denied any talks and accused the US president of lying about negotiations as cover while readying a ground invasion.
Trump warned that if a deal were not struck — including to reopen the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping lane — US forces would destroy "all of their Electric Generating Plants, Oil Wells and Kharg Island (and possibly all desalinisation plants!)."
Destroying civilian infrastructure would be illegal under international humanitarian law and could constitute a war crime, experts say.
Iran has previously threatened to retaliate by targeting energy infrastructure and desalination plants in its Arab neighbours that host the US military, such as the United Arab Emirates, Oman, Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi urged Saudi Arabia to "eject US forces", saying Tehran otherwise respects the "brotherly" kingdom.
Showing it will not back down, an Iranian parliamentary committee voted to impose tolls on vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, the passageway through which one-fifth of global oil passes.
State television said Iran would forbid the United States and Israel from passing through.
The tolling plan for the strait has outraged the United States, which has spoken of creating a "coalition" to oppose it.
"No one in the world can accept it," Secretary of State Marco Rubio told Al-Jazeera.
"It sets an incredible precedent. So this means that nations can now take over international waterways and claim them as their own," Rubio said of the waterway the US president recently called the "Strait of Trump."
- Peacekeepers die in Lebanon -
Israel has also relentlessly pounded Lebanon, including central Beirut, as it seeks to deliver a heavy blow to Hezbollah, the Iranian-backed force that had fired rockets in solidarity after Israeli forces killed Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
The UN mission in Lebanon said that two Indonesian peacekeepers were killed when "an explosion of unknown origin destroyed their vehicle", with two other peacekeepers wounded, one seriously.
Another Indonesian peacekeeper was killed on Sunday. Indonesia, the world's largest Muslim-majority country, before the latest war had been lining up to send forces to bring stability to ravaged Gaza following a ceasefire between Israel and Hamas.
France, a key player in Lebanon, condemned the deaths of peacekeepers and called for an urgent UN Security Council meeting, which was subsequently scheduled for Tuesday at 14H00 GMT.
Economy ministers and central bankers from the G7 club of rich countries meanwhile met in Paris to discuss the war's effects, with many countries introducing energy-saving measures or cutting fuel taxes to help consumers.
Market experts warned that any US ground operation or wider Iranian retaliation could send oil prices to levels not seen since the July 2008 commodity boom, when the cost of Brent crude, the international benchmark, hit close to $150 a barrel.
Brent has already risen nearly 60 percent this month, and the US benchmark WTI by more than half.
Adding pressure, Yemen's Iranian-backed Houthi rebels over the weekend fired missiles and drones at Israel, posing a threat to shipping on the Red Sea in addition to the Gulf.
- New strikes -
There was no let-up in hostilities.
Israel said its air defence batteries responded to missiles launched from Iran, after earlier announcing it was striking military infrastructure across Tehran.
Israel also confirmed it had hit the Imam Hossein University in the capital, which it said was used by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps for advanced weapons research.
In Israel, emergency services reported a fire at an oil refinery in the northern port city of Haifa, which also suffered a blaze on March 19.
Kuwait condemned strikes on a power station and a desalination plant, which killed an Indian worker.
Netanyahu said that Israel had achieved key objectives including by "wiping out" industrial plants and coming "close to finishing their arms industry."
"It's definitely beyond the halfway point. But I don't want to put a schedule on it," Netanyahu told US broadcaster Newsmax.
The war — and the spiraling price of oil — has been unpopular in the United States, where Rubio again Monday said it would last "weeks" more and not months.
- Egypt pleads for end -
Egyptian President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi, whose country is feeling the economic pinch and has been playing a key role mediating indirect talks, appealed directly to Trump on Monday to find an offramp.
"Please, help us to stop the war, you are capable of it," Sisi told a press conference.
Trump has claimed to be in direct contact with senior Iranian figures whom he did not identify publicly.
Rubio said there were "fractures" within the Islamic republic and voiced hope that the Iranian officials allegedly in contact with Washington had the "power to deliver."
But Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei again denied any negotiations, saying the United States had sent only a request to talk via intermediaries including Pakistan.
After weeks of strikes, residents of Tehran painted a picture of a city that is still clinging to some routine, with cafes and restaurants open and no shortages reported in supermarkets or petrol stations.
Security remains tight, with checkpoints erected on streets around the capital.
"When I make it to a cafe table, even for a few minutes, I can almost believe the world hasn't ended," said Fatemeh, 27, a dental assistant.
"And then I go back home, back to the reality of living through war, with all its darkness and weight."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singapore ICA warns of heavy traffic at Woodlands, Tuas checkpoints over long weekend
Article:
SINGAPORE: Singapore's Immigration and Checkpoints Authority (ICA) has warned of heavy congestion and longer clearance times at the Woodlands and Tuas checkpoints over the upcoming Good Friday and Qing Ming long weekend from Apr 3 to 5.
In an advisory posted on its official website, ICA said that, in view of the heightened global security situation, including recent developments in Middle East, it has stepped up security checks on arriving travellers, cargo, and conveyances at all checkpoints.
"Travellers should expect longer waiting times to clear immigration and are advised to check the traffic situation at the land checkpoints before embarking on their journey," it said on Monday.
According to ICA, the recent March school holiday and Hari Raya Aidilfitri weekend from March 13 to 22 saw more than five million travellers cross the Woodlands and Tuas checkpoints.
The immigration authority noted that traffic peaked on March 18, with more than 564,000 travellers crossing in a single day.
"During peak hours, car travellers experienced waiting times of up to three hours for immigration clearance due to traffic tailback from Malaysia.
"Travellers are strongly encouraged to consider taking cross-border bus services as an alternative to driving, to avoid congestion at the land checkpoints," it added. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NASA begins countdown to Apr 1 Moon launch
Article:
CAPE CANAVERAL: NASA began its two-day countdown Monday ahead of what is slated to be its first crewed moonshot in more than half a century, a long-anticipated loop around Earth's satellite that is to pave the way for future exploration.
The first window to launch from the Kennedy Space Centre in Florida opens Wednesday, Apr 1 at 6.24pm (2224 GMT), and NASA officials said the countdown began at 4.44pm.
"The vehicle is ready, the system is ready. The crew is ready," Amit Kshatriya, the US space agency's associate administrator, said in a briefing.
"Behind this flight stands a campaign," he continued, noting recently announced plans including constructing a lunar base.
If Wednesday's launch is cancelled or delayed for any reason, there are more liftoff opportunities through Apr 6.
As of Monday evening, NASA officials voiced confidence that engineering operations and final preparations were proceeding smoothly.
"We'll fly when this hardware is ready," launch director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson told journalists.
"But certainly all indications are right now we are in excellent, excellent shape."
The four astronauts set to carry out the Moon voyage – Americans Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover and Christina Koch along with Canadian colleague Jeremy Hansen – are in quarantine ahead of their journey. NASA officials said Monday evening that they would have the opportunity to have dinner with their families at a Florida beach house.
Cloudy with a chance of history
The odyssey will mark a series of firsts: the first time a woman, a person of colour and a non-American will venture on a Moon mission.
It is also the inaugural crewed flight of NASA's new lunar rocket, dubbed SLS.
The mammoth orange-and-white rocket is designed to allow the United States to repeatedly return to the Moon in years to come, with the goal of establishing a permanent base that will offer a stepping stone for further exploration.
But getting it off the ground has not been simple. The Artemis 2 mission was originally due to take off as early as February but repeated setbacks stalled that goal and even necessitated rolling the rocket back to its hangar for analysis and repairs.
And then there is the weather: as of Monday, NASA reported that the forecast shows "an 80 per cent chance of favourable weather conditions."
"Cloud coverage and potential for high winds on the ground" were their primary concerns, the agency said.
Teams are also monitoring solar weather.
"I'm just gonna make an appeal to the space gods, okay?" Kshatriya said to laughter on Monday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'First, they must stop the war': Iran sets conditions for talks with US, Israel
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Iran has made it clear that negotiations to end the ongoing conflict with Israel and the United States will only proceed if hostilities stop and credible guarantees are put in place to prevent future attacks.
Iranian Ambassador to Malaysia Valiollah Mohammadi Nasrabadi said Teheran remained open to peace talks, including those proposed by Pakistan and supported by Malaysia, but stressed that past experiences had made Iran cautious.
He said previous negotiations, including those held under Oman's mediation in Oman, Italy and Switzerland, had shown progress before being abruptly disrupted by military action from the US and Israel.
"We announced from the first day of the war, we announced that we are ready to negotiate.
"But with Pakistan's offer, we had bad previous experiences. We told every country: the doors of negotiations were open. But first, they must stop the war.
"Second, there must be a guarantee that they will never attack Iran again.
"Otherwise, even if there is a ceasefire, after six months they will attack again. We cannot accept that," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Valiollah said Iran also remained wary based on past Israeli action in the region, citing what he described as repeated violations of agreements against countries such as Syria, Palestine and Lebanon.
He also said Israel's security approach involved recurring cycles of conflict across the region.
Iran, he said, welcomed the mediation efforts by countries including Pakistan, Turkiye, Egypt and Oman, but maintained negotiations could only proceed under the two stated conditions.
"We told Pakistan, a neighbour and friendly country, that we have very good relations. Turkiye is the same. We told them to go ahead. If the other side accepts, we are ready," he said.
He added that Malaysia could play a constructive role by supporting diplomatic efforts and encouraging all parties to halt hostilities.
"His Excellency (Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim) mentioned Iran has the right to stop the war and secure guarantees for the future.
"At least, they must show good intentions for a ceasefire. We want lasting peace — not temporary peace," he said.
Valiollah said the duration of the conflict ultimately depended on the opposing side.
"They started this; they must stop. We did not start any war," he said, while expressing appreciation for Malaysia's stance.
"I would like to thank the government and people of Malaysia, and the non-governmental organisations here. I know they are under pressure from many sides, but
Alhamdulillah
, they are on what we believe is the right path.
"We do not want to harm any country. We want all countries to respect each other's rights. If there is a problem, it should be resolved through negotiation and diplomacy," he added.
On Sunday, Pakistan said it was preparing to host "meaningful talks" in the coming days to help end the conflict, even as tensions remain high.
Speaking after talks with regional counterparts, Pakistan's Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar said discussions had focused on ways to achieve an early and lasting end to the conflict, including the possibility of US-Iran talks in
Islamabad.
Anwar has also urged both the US and Iran to respond positively to Pakistan's proposal, describing it as timely and constructive whilst reaffirming Iran's right to defend its sovereignty under international law.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US Army reviewing helicopters near Kid Rock's house and anti-Trump protests
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US Army said on Monday it was investigating why military helicopters were operating near the house of singer Kid Rock, seemingly in support of the vocal backer of US president Donald Trump, and also near anti-Trump protests.
Over the weekend, Rock posted a video on X, showing two Army Apache helicopters flying next to his house in Nashville, Tennessee.
The singer, standing in front of his swimming pool, can be seen saluting one of the helicopters.
Military helicopters were also seen flying close to demonstrators in the city, who were taking part in protests decrying Trump's aggressive deportation efforts, war in Iran, and other policies.
"Army aviators must adhere to strict safety standards, professionalism, and established flight regulations.
An administrative review is underway to assess the mission and verify compliance with regulations and airspace requirements," US Army spokesperson Major Montrell Russell said.
"Appropriate action will be taken if any violations are found. Until the review is complete, there will be no further comment," Russell added.
The US military is supposed to be apolitical, loyal to the US Constitution, and independent of any party or political movement.
US defence secretary Pete Hegseth has moved quickly to reshape the military, firing top generals and admirals as he seeks to implement Trump's national security agenda and root out diversity initiatives he calls discriminatory.
Democratic lawmakers have warned the Trump administration is increasingly trying to use the military for political gain.
In his post about the helicopters, Rock took a swipe at Democratic California governor Gavin Newsom.
"God Bless America and all those who have made the ultimate sacrifice to defend her," Rock commented above the video of the helicopters.
Over the weekend, as part of the anti-Trump "No Kings" protests, more than 3,200 events had been planned in all 50 states, after the two previous nationwide events attracted millions of participants.
Local news said thousands took part in Nashville as part of the protests.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian ships allowed safe passage through Strait of Hormuz by Iran
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  At least seven Malaysian-owned vessels are set to sail safely through the Strait of Hormuz within days despite escalating tensions in the region, following assurances from Iran.
Iranian Ambassador to Malaysia Valiollah Mohammadi Nasrabadi said Teheran had been informed of the vessels' presence in the Persian Gulf and their intention to pass through the strategic waterway.
"We received information from Malaysia's foreign minister that several Malaysian ships are in the Persian Gulf and want to pass through.
"We have considered this, and
InshaAllah
they will pass. It is no problem as Malaysia is a friendly country, and friendly countries can use the strait," he said in an exclusive interview with the
New Straits Times
at the Iranian Embassy here yesterday.
Asked whether Malaysian vessels would be subject to toll charges, Valiollah said they would be allowed to transit the strait free of charge.
"Of course (Malaysian ships are free to pass). We will not charge," he added.
On Saturday, Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan said seven Malaysian-owned tankers were awaiting clearance to transit the Strait of Hormuz.
The vessels comprised three owned by Petronas, two by MISC Bhd, and one each by Sapura Energy and Shapadu Corporation Sdn Bhd.
He said the ships were not being detained, but were awaiting a safe window and an official signal to proceed. Four of the vessels are reportedly laden with crude oil.
The restrictions on vessel movements in the strait came after Iran limited transit through the waterway — a key global choke point through which about one-fifth of the world's oil supply normally passes — in retaliation for  strikes launched by the United States and Israel about a month ago.
Meanwhile, Valiollah said the Strait of Hormuz remained open to international shipping, describing its selective restrictions as a lawful response to recent attacks while dismissing claims of a full closure.
He said access to the strategic waterway was being selectively restricted, with countries involved in or supporting attacks against Iran barred.
Iran was continuing to allow passage for friendly nations, subject to coordination with its authorities, he added.
He emphasised that the move did not constitute a closure of the strait, but a targeted response grounded in international law.
"I want to emphasise: the Strait of Hormuz is not closed. It is restricted for some, not all," he said.
"If you attack Iran, you cannot use this strait. But we did not close it."
Iran has been under US-led sanctions since 1979, following its Islamic Revolution and subsequent diplomatic fallout, with restrictions expanding over the decades to cover its energy, financial and shipping sectors.
Despite this, the Strait of Hormuz had remained open to international shipping prior to the latest conflict. Its closure  has led to a surge in global fuel prices.
Valiollah also dismissed the prospect of foreign naval intervention in the area, following reports that the US had sought support from North Atlantic Treaty Organisation (Nato) member countries to escort vessels through the strait.
He said there had been no meaningful response from Nato, adding that any such presence would be viewed by Teheran as support for US aggression.
On the rising global fuel prices, Valiollah said Iran had historically refrained from closing the strait despite decades of sanctions, citing concerns over the wider impact on global markets.
"Iran has been under sanctions for 47 years, but we have never closed the Strait of Hormuz. We could have done it, but we didn't.
"If we closed it, people around the world would suffer. We have problems with the US, but we do not want to extend those problems to other countries or global markets."
However, he indicated that the current situation marked a shift in approach, with the aggressor countries to blame for the surge in fuel prices.
"But now it is different. If someone attacks your home and then wants to use your straits, you will say no."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teacher wounded in Texas high school shooting
Article:
HOUSTON: A 15-year-old student died by suicide Monday after shooting and wounding a teacher at a school in Texas, authorities said.
The incident took place at Hill Country College Preparatory High School in Comal County, outside San Antonio in south-central Texas.
"A teacher was shot by a student and transported to a San Antonio hospital. At this time, we have no updated information on her condition. The student involved, a 15-year-old, died at the scene," the Comal County Sheriff's Office posted on Facebook.
"The student shot the teacher and then we believe he turned the gun on himself and shot himself," Sheriff Mark Reynolds said at a press conference, adding that the situation was quickly contained.
The high school was placed on lockdown and students were transferred to a nearby school to reunite with their parents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King briefed on Onsa by Fahmi
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
HIS Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, was briefed yesterday on the Online Safety Act 2025 (Onsa) at Istana Bukit Tunku here.
According to a post on Sultan Ibrahim's Facebook page, Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil presented details of the act.
Fahmi said the act sought to address fake accounts targeting Malay rulers and the royal families.
It also aims to make the Internet safer for families and children by establishing clear responsibilities for online platforms.
This includes a proposed age verification mechanism to prevent children under 16 years old from creating social media accounts.
Sultan Ibrahim also granted an audience to Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim at the palace.
According to another Facebook post, the king was briefed on the latest developments in the federal government's administration.
They also discussed several current national issues.
The audience is part of regular meetings between the king and prime minister to ensure the smooth running of government administration.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelantan rivers greener but it may be a bad sign
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
RIVERS in Kelantan are turning green due to the prolonged dry weather and ecosystem stress, not because of improvement in water quality.
Universiti Malaysia Kelantan Climate, Water and Environmental Resilience Research Group head Associate Professor Dr Mohamad Faiz Mohd Amin said the phenomenon was linked to a lack of rainfall.
He said during the rainy season, Sungai Kelantan carries a high load of suspended sediment from erosion, giving the water a murky brown appearance.
"However, reduced rainfall slows water flow and limits erosion, significantly lowering the amount of sediment levels. This physical change is often mistaken for an improvement in water quality."
Faiz added that clearer water allowed sunlight to penetrate deeper, highlighting the natural colours in the aquatic system, including phytoplankton or algae, to a limited extent.
He said rivers like Sungai Kelantan were not prone to large-scale algal blooms.
"The green colour is more likely linked to reduced sediment rather than algae."
On Sungai Golok, where water levels have dropped to 3.26m from the usual 5m, Faiz said the lower volume reduced the river's ability to dilute pollutants.
"When water volume decreases, substances, such as nutrients from agricultural runoff or waste discharge, become more concentrated and water temperatures rise. This affects water quality and places stress on aquatic life.
"It can also threaten raw water supplies."
He said the situation in the state pointed to a potential water security crisis driven by climate change, with increasingly erratic rainfall patterns and longer droughts.
"Rivers that look more 'beautiful' today may actually be losing their natural ability to support life.
"Water resource management must evolve to be more adaptive, data-driven and climate-responsive," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Attack on Michigan synagogue was Hizbollah-inspired 'act of terrorism,' says FBI
Article:
WASHINGTON: The FBI said on Monday that an attack on the largest Jewish temple in Michigan earlier this month was an "act of terrorism" inspired by Hizbollah.
Ayman Ghazali, a 41-year-old man who was born in Lebanon and became a US citizen in 2016, killed himself during the March 12 attack, when he crashed his truck into the Temple of Israel synagogue before opening fire on security guards and causing an explosion using fireworks, said Jennifer Runyan, the special agent in charge of the Federal Bureau of Investigation's Detroit field office.
No one else died during the attack on the synagogue where children were attending preschool.
Ghazali consumed pro-Hizbollah ideology prior to the attack, said Runyan, but the FBI has not been able to verify if he was a member of Hizbollah. There is no evidence that he had co-conspirators, Runyan said.
Lebanese armed group Hizbollah was founded by Iran's elite Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps in 1982. Both Hizbollah and the IRGC are designated as foreign terrorist organisations by the US. The US and Israel launched a war against Iran on February 28.
"Had this man lived, I am convinced that my office would prove beyond a reasonable doubt that he committed the federal crime of providing material support to Hizbollah," said Jerome Borgen, the US attorney for the Eastern District of Michigan.
Runyan said the day before the synagogue attack Ghazali started sharing photos on social media of Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in US-Israeli strikes last month.
Then, on the day of the attack, while sitting in the parking lot of the Temple of Israel, Ghazali told his sister in a message that he planned "to commit a mass terrorist attack."
Antisemitic incidents have spiked in recent years in the US, with anti-Jewish incidents accounting for nearly two-thirds of 5,300-plus religiously motivated hate crimes since February 2024, according to FBI data.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Overweight' call on consumer products sector
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: The consumer products sector is expected to maintain earnings resilience and visibility, backed by stable domestic consumption and fiscal support.
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) said measures such as Sumbangan Asas Rahmah and targeted fuel subsidies should help sustain consumer spending amid rising global energy prices.
"Under Budi Madani RON95, while the monthly quota is reduced to 200 litres, 90 per cent of users consume below this level, implying limited immediate impact on the mass market.
"Overall, we do not expect any near-term policy shifts that could materially disrupt consumption trends. However, we remain watchful that a prolonged Middle East conflict could pose second-order risks via inflation," it said in a note.
The research house said nine out of 17 stocks under its coverage met earnings expectations for the fourth quarter of 2025, while seven disappointed and one exceeded expectations.
During the quarter, it downgraded Padini Holdings Bhd due to persistently weak sales momentum.
The sector saw a pick-up in consumer spending towards the year-end, particularly among discretionary players, driven by favourable seasonality.
This also suggests a steady consumption trend in line with improving consumer sentiment.
RHB Research maintained its "overweight" call on the sector, with Nestle (M) Bhd, Mr DIY Group (M) Bhd, Eco-Shop Marketing Bhd, Farm Fresh Bhd and Aeon Co (M) Bhd as its top picks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar's junta chief nears presidency in power shift
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
NAYPYIDAW: Myanmar's junta chief Min Aung Hlaing was nominated as a vice-presidential candidate and replaced as military commander yesterday, paving the way for the coup leader to become president and continue his rule in civilian garb.
Min Aung Hlaing has ruled Myanmar since 2021 when he ordered a coup toppling the elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi —  detaining the Nobel Peace Prize laureate, dissolving her party and triggering civil war.
After five years of hardline rule, he oversaw heavily restricted elections that criminalised protest or criticism of the vote and returned a walkover win for pro-military parties in late January.
Democracy watchdogs have long warned that the government will be a proxy of the military, which has ruled Myanmar for the vast majority of its post-independence history.
"I nominate Senior General Min Aung Hlaing as vice-president," said member of parliament Kyaw Kyaw Htay, according to a television broadcast of a lower house session on state-run media.
Three vice-presidents will be chosen, one of whom will be elected as president in a Parliament-wide vote.
Myanmar's junta also installed a new military commander-in-chief, with former spymaster Ye Win Oo replacing Min Aung Hlaing, said reports.
Ye Win Oo was promoted to the top military role at a ceremony here, several media outlets reported.
Myanmar's military has long presented itself as the only force guarding the restive country from rupture and ruin.
The generals loosened their grip for a decade-long democratic interlude beginning in 2011, allowing Suu Kyi to ascend as civilian leader and steer a period of reform as the nation opened up from its hermetic history.
After Suu Kyi trounced the pro-military Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) with a landslide victory in the 2020 elections, Min Aung Hlaing snatched back power as he grew anxious about the military's waning influence, said analysts.
Now the USDP is entrenched in Parliament after winning 80 per cent of elected seats, and it is expected that the new government will march in lockstep with the military.
However, Min Aung Hlaing must manage a delicate transition as he hands over the reins of the all-powerful military to Ye Win Oo as he moves into a civilian role. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Panama Canal sees traffic boost over Iran war
Article:
PANAMA CITY: The war in the Middle East has prompted a surge in ships utilising the Panama Canal, an executive for the interoceanic waterway said Monday.
The month-long conflict that began on Feb 28 has seen Iran effectively blockade the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of world oil and gas exports normally transit.
"We had expected around 34 daily passages" for this year, but in the last two weeks "we've been having 38, 39, 40," the deputy administrator of the canal, Ilya Espino de Marotta, told the channel Telemetro in an interview.
Five per cent of global maritime trade passes through the Panama Canal, whose main users are the United States and China. The route primarily connects the east coast of the United States with the Asian giants, South Korea, and Japan.
"The Panama Canal is a safe, short route, that with gas prices" still reduces costs, Espino de Marotta said.
Nonetheless, the canal executive warned that the passage of 40 daily boats "isn't sustainable" given the limited space on the route.
She added that by April, the canal should see renewed growth in the number of boats carrying liquified natural gas (LNG), after a slump due to Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
"We were going over 500 passages a year," but then it dropped away "because of the war in Ukraine and Russia, though it's now coming back," Espino de Marotta said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australian regulator probes Facebook, YouTube over teen social media ban
Article:
SYDNEY: A host of tech giants including Facebook and YouTube are under investigation for potentially breaching Australia's world-leading social media ban for under-16s, the nation's online watchdog said Tuesday.
"While social media platforms have taken some initial action, I am concerned through our compliance monitoring that some may not be doing enough to comply with Australian law," eSafety commissioner Julie Inman Grant said.
Australia in December banned users under 16 from the world's largest social media platforms, citing the need to protect them from "predatory algorithms" and online bullying.
Inman Grant said there were "significant concerns" that Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, TikTok and YouTube may have breached the ban.
"As a result, we are now moving into an enforcement stance," she said.
Companies falling under the ban face fines of up to US$33 million if they are found to be flouting the laws.
"They can choose to do so or face escalating consequences, including profound reputational erosion with governments and consumers globally," said Inman Grant.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Netanyahu says Iran war goals achieved 'beyond halfway point'
Article:
WASHINGTON: Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Monday that the war on Iran had achieved more than half its aims, without putting a timeline on when it would end.
"It's definitely beyond the halfway point. But I don't want to put a schedule on it," Netanyahu told the conservative US broadcaster Newsmax.
He added that he meant the war was more than halfway "in terms of missions, not necessarily in terms of time."
US president Donald Trump, who launched the war with Netanyahu on February 28, initially said the operation would last for four to six weeks.
US secretary of state Marco Rubio said Monday that the war would last "weeks" further instead of months, amid wide US public opposition to a conflict that has sent oil prices soaring.
Netanyahu said the war had achieved goals including killing "thousands" of members of Iran's Revolutionary Guards.
Israel and the United States are also "close to finishing their arms industry," he added.
"Just the whole industrial base – wiping out all, you know, just plants, entire plants, and the nuclear programme itself," he said.
Netanyahu and Trump have repeatedly alleged that Iran was close to building a nuclear weapon, a claim not supported by the UN nuclear watchdog and that comes despite Trump saying he "obliterated" key sites in attacks last year.
Netanyahu also voiced confidence that Iran's islamic republic would fall, though he said again that this was not the goal.
"I think this regime will collapse internally. But at the moment, right now, what we're doing is just degrading their military capacity, degrading their missile capacity, degrading their nuclear capacity and also weakening them from the inside," Netanyahu said.
The war came weeks after Iranian authorities clamped down on major anti-government demonstrations, killing thousands of people.
Trump in recent days has said the war has effectively achieved "regime change" by empowering other figures within the cleric-run state who Trump says may be more accommodating.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MMA urges action on drink-driving crashes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Medical Association (MMA) has urged the government to take decisive reforms to curb the rise in fatal accidents linked to driving under the influence (DUI).
MMA president Datuk Dr Thirunavukarasu Rajoo said even a single death caused by DUI was unacceptable, stressing that the recent fatal crash in Klang, which claimed the life of a motorcyclist, was not an isolated incident.
He said although official data show DUI contributes to less than 0.5 per cent of fatal road accidents in Malaysia, each case represents an irreversible loss with lasting consequences for families.
"DUI is not a seasonal issue but a persistent national risk that requires a comprehensive and coordinated response.
"Evidence from the Malaysian Institute of Road Safety Research (Miros) shows that a drunk driver is 13 times more likely to cause a crash.
"This risk is further compounded when alcohol and drugs are used concurrently, making DUI not a marginal issue but a clear and avoidable threat to public safety," he said in a statement today.
Despite stricter penalties for DUI-linked offences under amendments to the Road Transport Act 2020, including imprisonment and hefty fines, he said a critical gap remains in support for victims' families.
He said financial penalties imposed on offenders do not directly benefit affected families, many of whom are left to navigate lengthy legal processes, insurance disputes and financial hardship without adequate assistance.
"Malaysia currently lacks a dedicated compensation mechanism for victims of DUI, resulting in families being 'failed twice' — First by the incident, and then by the system meant to deliver justice.
"Justice must not stop at punishment. It must extend to protection, support and dignity for the families left behind," he added.
Hence, Dr Thirunavukarasu said systemic reforms were needed, including the establishment of a dedicated DUI victim compensation fund to provide timely financial assistance, as well as mandatory legal aid.
He said enforcement must also be strengthened through sustained, year-round operations with high-visibility and unpredictable roadblocks, including routine screening for both alcohol and drugs.
"Preventive measures must also be enhanced by lowering the permissible blood alcohol concentration (BAC) limit in line with World Health Organisation recommendations.
"We should also mandate rehabilitation programmes for offenders, and implement continuous nationwide public education campaigns on the dangers of DUI," he added.
Yesterday, a man was killed in an accident along Jalan Raya Barat in Klang when a man driving under the influence of alcohol crashed into his motorcycle. The driver also tested positive for drugs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 3 more unlicensed photographers to be charged tomorrow over KLCC attack
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Three of five unlicensed photographers suspected to be involved in an attack on two individuals at Persiaran Menara Berkembar Petronas (KLCC) on March 22 will be charged tomorrow.
Dang Wangi district police chief ACP Sazalee Adam said the three will be charged under Regulation 25(1)(n) of the National Registration Regulations 1990 for not possessing identification documents as Malaysian citizens, adding that two other suspects in the attack have been charged today under Section 149 and 324 of the Penal Code.
The five photographers were arrested around KLCC along Jalan Amapng on March 22 to facilitate investigations into the attack.
The attack was believed to have been triggered when the victim saw and approached a group of men beating an Indian tourist.
When the victim tried to confront the men and disperse them, they were attacked suddenly from behind with an individual using a kerambit knife. —  BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police seek assistance in locating missing Palestinian man
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are seeking public assistance to look for a missing Palestinian man last seen at Bukit Mewah Apartments, Taman Bukit, Kajang, Selangor on March 23.
Kajang district police chief ACP Naazron Abdul Yusof said the man, Al Baraa Monir Sobhi Alqedra, 22, is 180 centimetres tall and weighs 70 kilogrammes.
"He was last seen at the location at 8 am wearing a yellow t-shirt and black long pants.
"The Kajang police district headquarters received a report about the missing person at 5.56 pm, March 24," he said in a statement here today.
Those with information are asked to contact the Kajang police district headquarters at  03-89114222 of Kajang police station chief, Insp Shisheewady Ruzani at 016-8565670.
– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Syariah-based 'diyat' proposed as justice mechanism for drink-driving victim's family
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The 'diyat' mechanism as a Syariah-based compensation should be explored as an instrument of justice for the family of Amirul Hafiz Omar, who was killed in a suspected drink-driving crash at Jalan Raya Barat, Klang, Selangor, yesterday, says Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Religious Affairs) Dr Zulkifli Hasan.
He said the rights of the victim's heirs must not be overlooked and that the diyat approach should be given due consideration to ensure justice and proper protection for the bereaved family.
"I strongly condemn the tragic incident in Klang, which claimed the life of a father, Amirul Hafiz Omar, due to a driver believed to be intoxicated and positive for drugs. Driving under the influence not only violates the law but also endangers the lives of others.
"This incident is deeply saddening and represents a form of negligence that cannot be compromised with, as it involves the loss of an invaluable life," he said in a Facebook post today.
Diyat is a system of monetary compensation to a victim's family where death has occurred either intentionally or accidentally.
Zulkifli said that a discussion regarding the implementation of diyat was initiated on Feb 11 in Putrajaya, involving muftis, prominent figures, and experts under the Prime Minister's Department (Religious Affairs).
He said the effort was aimed at ensuring justice and protecting the rights of heirs, as well as supporting the implementation of diyat.
Meanwhile, he welcomed the statement by the Ministry of Transport, which was drafting amendments to Section 44 of the Road Transport Act 1987 to require offenders to pay compensation to victims' families in addition to imprisonment.
"May justice be upheld, and may this incident serve as a reminder to all road users. May the soul of the late Amirul Hafiz Omar be placed among the righteous," he said.
Yesterday, South Klang District Police chief ACP Lim Jit Huey said a motorcyclist was killed after being hit by a car driven by a person under the influence of alcohol and drugs in a crash on Jalan Raya Barat in Klang.
The incident, which occurred at about 11.40 am, resulted in the victim being flung onto the roof of another car and he was pronounced dead at the scene.
A video recording of the accident also went viral on social media, showing a speeding car overtaking on a double line and crashing into a motorcycle, causing the rider to be tossed into the air before landing on another vehicle.
Meanwhile, Federation of Peninsular Malay Students (GPMS) secretary-general Wafiyuddin Musa, in a statement, agreed with the proposal, saying that diyat should be expanded as a mechanism for immediate compensation to victims' families regardless of race and religion.
He said the mechanism was more effective than the existing practice, which required victims' families to file civil claims to obtain compensation, a process that usually involved high costs and a lengthy period.
"GPMS proposes that a mandatory compensation mechanism be introduced for the families of victims involved in fatal accidents caused by drivers under the influence of alcohol or drugs," he said.
He said GPMS was also prepared to offer pro bono legal services to victims' families to assist in any appropriate legal action.
Meanwhile, Wafiyuddin said GPMS also proposed that the designated driver concept be strengthened through national campaigns and government policy support to ensure individuals who consumed alcohol did not drive.
"In Malaysia, concepts such as Buddy Driver by Trevo can be enhanced as part of efforts to cultivate safe driving. This step is important to ensure that individuals who consume alcohol do not drive, thereby reducing the risk of fatal crashes," he said.
-- BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police probing man caught taking 'upskirt' photos of women in Subang mall
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have launched an investigation into a man suspected of taking voyeuristic 'upskirt' photographs of women at a retail outlet in Subang Jaya.
Subang Jaya district police chief Assistant Commissioner Wan Azlan Wan Mamat confirmed that a report has been lodged regarding the incident, which gained traction after a CCTV recording of the act began circulating online.
"Police confirm receiving a report regarding the viral video.
"Investigations are currently underway to track down and identify both the victims and the suspects involved.
"Further updates will be provided in due course," Wan Azlan said in a statement today.
The footage, which captures the suspect's illicit actions, has triggered widespread public outrage on social media, with netizens calling for swift enforcement action against the individual.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zara Qairina's inquest resumes tomorrow
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Coroner's Court inquest into the death of Zara Qairina Mahathir is set to resume tomorrow, with the court expected to call another child witness to testify.
Since proceedings began in September last year, a total of 64 witnesses have been called. The latest witness to testify, on Feb 3, was the headmistress of Sekolah Agama Negeri Naluyan, Sipitang, Nur Hidayah Mohamad.
Nur Hidayah, a relative of the deceased, provided testimony regarding Zara Qairina's character and academic life at the religious school prior to her enrolment in Form One at Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Agama (SMKA) Tun Datu Mustapha, Limauan Papar.
Following a two-month hiatus for the Aidilfitri holidays, the inquest will continue tomorrow, with several expert witnesses also expected to take the stand starting April 1.
The mother of the deceased, Noraidah Lamat, is expected to be the final witness to testify before the Coroner's Court delivers its verdict on the cause of death.
To date, the court has heard from a diverse range of witnesses, including school principals, wardens, teachers, security guards, and investigating police officers.
Medical experts and officers involved in treating Zara Qairina and conducting the post-mortem — following the exhumation of her remains on Aug 9 — have also testified.
Additionally, more than 30 students, including Zara Qairina's hostel mates, provided testimony in a closed court between September and January.
Zara Qairina was found unconscious on the ground floor of her hostel building in the early hours of July 16 last year. She succumbed to severe traumatic brain injury on July 17 while receiving treatment at the Queen Elizabeth Hospital.
She was initially buried on the same day; however, her remains were exhumed on Aug 9 for an autopsy following an application by her mother's legal team.
In the proceedings before Coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan, the mother is represented by a legal team comprising Shahlan Jufri, Datuk Rizwandean M. Borhan, Mohd Syarulnizam Mohd Salleh, Mohd Luqman Syazwan Zabidi, Farrah Nasser, and Elhanan James.
The father, Mahathir Hashim @ Marikin, is represented by Sherzali Asli, Nurul Rafeeqa Afdul Mutolip, and Clarice Vyonne Conrad.
Datuk Ram Singh, Joan Goh, and Elmer Prem Ganasan are appearing for a student identified as 'Student A,' who faces charges in the Children's Court.
Four other students — Students B, C, D, and E — who also face charges in the Children's Court, are represented by Azhier Farhan Arisin, Abdul Fikry Jaafar, Farah Nazriah Chun Lee, Fareez Salleh, and Sylyester Kuan.
The inquest is being conducted by Sabah State Prosecution Director Nahra Dollah, Deputy Head of Prosecution II at the Attorney-General's Chambers (AGC) Datuk Badius Zaman Ahmad, and Deputy Public Prosecutor Mohd Fairuz Johari.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Families of drink-driving victims deserve commensurate compensation, says Syefura
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Road safety laws must be amended to ensure that families of victims involved in accidents caused by drink-drivers receive more equitable compensation, said Bentong Member of Parliament Young Syefura Othman.
She expressed her support for such legislative changes and emphasised the urgent need for stricter enforcement, particularly in high-risk areas or "hotspots."
Young Syefura noted that despite previous amendments to the Road Transport Act 1987 in 2020, drink-driving cases resulting in death and injury continue to persist.
"Based on the latest data following the previous amendments, 2,306 cases were recorded in 2022, followed by 1,989 cases in 2023, and 1,589 cases in 2024.
"For the period of January to June 2025, a total of 508 cases have been reported," she said in a post on Threads today.
"I agree with the proposed amendments, such as providing commensurate compensation to victims' families and enforcing stricter measures in hotspot areas.
"This is an inter-ministerial issue that requires full coordination between ministries to prevent such tragedies from recurring."
The lawmaker also expressed her deepest sympathies and condolences to the family of a victim involved in a fatal accident in Klang yesterday.
In the incident, a motorcyclist was killed after being rammed by a car driven by a man under the influence of alcohol and drugs.
Klang South District Police Chief Assistant Commissioner Lim Jit Huey confirmed that the 11.40am crash involved a motorcycle and two cars.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: E-commerce in the crosshairs at WTO in digital taxes battle
Article:
THE    future of digital taxes is dividing countries at the World Trade Organisation (WTO), with the moratorium that has prohibited customs duties on electronic transmissions since 1998 front and centre in the debate.
The moratorium is highly important for developed countries — notably for the United States, which is calling for it to be made permanent rather than kept under regular review.
So far, only India has openly voiced disagreement with renewing the moratorium, according to several sources close to the discussions in Yaounde at the WTO's ministerial conference, the organisation's biennial supreme decision-making body.
"There is only one country that's been vocally not supporting," said a Western diplomatic source.
"Normally, it's kind of a handful of countries, whereas it's only been one so far this time."
But since decisions are made by consensus at the WTO, exerting pressure on this issue could be a way for India to gain concessions elsewhere.
WTO members generally apply tariffs to imported goods and services, but in 1998, they agreed not to impose them on e-commerce.
"The rule is to have no tariffs on what circulates via the Internet," said Valerie Picard, an official with the International Chamber of Commerce, who is attending the conference.
"So when you download software, when an SME uses the cloud, when a freelancer sells a design service abroad, there are no taxes at the border," she said.
"The moratorium applies to everything that is digital. It goes far beyond digital books and music. It also includes, for example, security updates, online courses, telemedicine," she added.
From 1998 onwards, the temporary moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions has been renewed at successive WTO ministerial conferences.
However, discussions were particularly tense at the last ministerial meeting in 2024 in Abu Dhabi.
At the last minute, India agreed not to veto an extension — but only for a maximum of two years.
In the absence of a common understanding on the scope, "the continued extension of this moratorium warrants careful reconsideration", India's Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal said recently.
The moratorium is set to expire today  unless ministers in Yaounde decide otherwise.
The African, Caribbean and Pacific Group of States at the WTO is proposing to keep the moratorium until the next ministerial conference.
The US, supported by several countries including Japan, Mexico, Australia, Norway and Switzerland, wants to make the moratorium permanent.
"The United States is not interested in another temporary extension of the moratorium," US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said recently.
Approving an open-ended moratorium "will deliver stability and predictability for all traders", while showing that the WTO can deliver results, said Joseph Barloon, the US ambassador to the organisation.
To limit opposition to the moratorium, the US under President Donald Trump has negotiated a clause in recent bilateral agreements with certain countries, notably Indonesia.
"The worst case would, of course, be that we don't have an extension of the moratorium.
"That's something that we cannot exclude," the Swiss WTO ambassador Erwin Bollinger said ahead of the conference.
Some developing countries are more reticent about the moratorium because they see it as a loss of tax revenue and argue that the rapid pace of digital transformation only exacerbates the problem.
According to a 2023 study published by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, the potential budgetary impact of the moratorium is limited, "amounting to, on average, 0.68 per cent of total customs revenue or 0.1 per cent of total government revenue".
The OECD also noted that in most countries, the shortfall could be offset by increased VAT revenue from imports of digital services.
The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Missed your SPM targets? It's not the end, says career consultant
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: With Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) results due to be released tomorrow, a tertiary education consultant is offering free guidance to school-leavers who may not have performed as expected, helping them explore alternative study pathways and career options.
Education consultant Thilaa Paneer Selvam, who has 10 years of experience in education consultancy and industry placement, said students should not feel discouraged by their results.
"Every year, we see students who think their future is over just because they didn't get the results they hoped for. That is simply not true," she said.
Drawing on her decade-long track record, Thilaa said she has helped many students transition into stable careers despite modest academic results.
"Many were not high academic achievers initially, but they found their strengths and are now doing well in their respective fields," she said.
She said her initiative provides free one-on-one consultations, focusing on pathways such as Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET), diploma programmes and skills-based courses.
Working closely with a number of universities and colleges, Thilaa said students who complete their programmes are guided towards job placements through industry-linked pathways.
Among the courses offered are Culinary Arts, Hotel Management, Electrical and Electronics, Renewable Solar Energy and Early Childhood Education, all accredited by the Malaysian Qualifications Agency (MQA).
"We don't just look at what to study, but where it can lead. It's about aligning education with long-term career opportunities," she said.
Thilaa noted that many parents remain unaware of alternative pathways and tend to focus on conventional university entry.
"Parents play a crucial role. Success is not defined by straight A's alone. There are many successful individuals who took different routes," she said.
She added that the consultations include aptitude assessments, career matching and guidance on suitable institutions based on students' interests and strengths.
"A large part of our focus is on students from the interiors. We provide free accommodation and allowances so they can pursue their studies without financial burden, and ensure they are placed in jobs upon completion," she said.
Thilaa encouraged school-leavers to explore their options early.
"There should not be any shame. What matters is what you do next," she said.
'Those seeking tertiary education consultation may contact 017-311 9827 or visit  her social media handle at Thilaa. Official.'
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: Is Malaysia heading for a rice  crisis?
Article:
RICE has been a staple food for the people in Asia, including Malaysians, for thousand of years.  The growth of the tin mining and plantation industries in British Malaya then drove its consumption.
Unfortunately, the British neglected rice cultivation in favour of tin and rubber production, resulting in complacent production that continues to this day. Colonial laws ensured rice cultivation was done exclusively by the Malays until 1939. Post-Independence, rice production surged  following the Green Revolution and infrastructural modernisation through the Muda Irrigation Project (1966–1970).
The adoption of new technologies enabled Kedah and Perlis to double-crop annually and become the nation's rice bowl.   Indigenous tribes believe in the "rice soul", a "spirit" that's reverently treated, especially during harvest celebrations.
Transcending calories and nutrients, rice is now a civilisational engine that organises human life, thought and essential governance, administering and retailing 9,000 tonnes daily and more than 3.25 million tonnes annually. Driven by rapid urbanisation, rice consumption grew two per cent on a year-over-year basis, consistent with Malaysia's population growth of 7.18 million in 1957 to 35 million in 2025.
The country produces 60 to 70 per cent of its needs but imports the rest from Vietnam, Thailand, Pakistan, India and Cambodia. Imports are set  to spike further when the population grows to 43 million in 2059.
The implication now is that while imports rise, local production continually lags.    Added to this dire mix are ageing farmers, reduced cultivation land, drought and other climate change-induced water supply issues, high production costs, seed availability and rising diesel prices.
These all aggravate massive land idleness, not helped by a rice supply monopoly that makes farming unattractive and non-viable.
Lately, padi farmers have been worried by the Muda Agricultural Development Authority's push for dry direct seeding for the upcoming planting season.  The method reduces water use, but without farmer training, soil management and mechanisation support, it risks depressing yields and farmer confidence.   Yields, the farmers warned, may drop by 80 per cent.
In response, the government has pledged to increase the national rice buffer stock to 300,000 tonnes, supervised by the National Padi and Rice Industry Consultative Committee, to maintain staple security. While these initiatives cushion short-term shocks, they are unsustainable if domestic production weakens.
In the meantime, the rice authorities could look to India, China and Japan for modern sustainable farming techniques. Investments should go to drought and heat-resistant rice varieties and  planting calendars should be based on climate data. Large-scale padi projects should also be expanded and  five seasons in two years should be introduced.
The alternative option is to simply increase imports, a huge strain on the national coffers.   The unsettling economic and culinary repercussions? Prices of the indispensable nasi lemak, nasi ayam and nasi kandar will only continue to soar.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ceiling fares for tour buses and vans implemented following surge in diesel prices
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Inbound Tourism Association (Mita) has implemented new ceiling fares for tour buses and vans, effective immediately, to mitigate the impact of surging diesel prices on operators and travellers.
The association stated that the adjustment was necessary as tour vehicles and ferries have been excluded from the national diesel subsidy mechanism since the abolition of the blanket subsidy in 2024, Free Malaysia Today reported.
Under the new structure, tour bus rates are set between RM1,100 for series tours and up to RM2,200 for domestic day tours.
Van fares have been capped at RM900 for series tours.
Long-distance travel from Kuala Lumpur will now cost RM2,400 for trips to Kelantan, while journeys to Penang and Ipoh are priced at RM1,900.
These rates are scheduled to remain in effect until Wednesday (April 1).
Mita President Mint Leong described the move as a "quick win" to ensure the survival of tourism players while maintaining safe and reliable transport services.
"We had to take this step to ensure tourism players can survive and continue serving customers," Leong said at a press conference today.
Leong also urged the government to reconsider providing diesel subsidy support for the industry, proposing monthly quotas of 3,000 litres for buses and 2,500 litres for vans.
"We are aware that securing such subsidies is challenging; our previous applications were rejected," she said.
Diesel prices have climbed by RM2.40 to approximately RM5.52 per litre over the last three weeks, a spike attributed to global energy supply constraints due to the Middle East conflict.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran war raises 2028 stakes as Trump weighs Vance vs Rubio
Article:
AS the war in Iran threatens to imperil President Donald Trump's legacy, the political stakes also are rising for two of his top lieutenants: Vice-President J.D. Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
The pair, widely viewed as potential successors to Trump, have been thrust into still-developing negotiations to end the war at a moment when the Republican Party is already weighing its post-Trump future.
Vance has taken a cautious approach, reflecting his scepticism towards prolonged United States military involvement, while Rubio has aligned himself closely with Trump's hawkish stance and emerged as one of the administration's most vocal defenders of the campaign.
Trump has said both men were involved in efforts to force Iran to accept US demands to dismantle its nuclear and ballistic missile programmes and allow oil traffic to pass freely through the Strait of Hormuz.
With the next presidential election due in 2028 and term limits barring Trump from running again, the president has been putting the succession question to allies and advisers in private, asking "J.D. or Marco?" two people familiar with his views said.
The outcome of the US military operation now in its fifth week could shape the two men's 2028 prospects, political analysts and Republican officials said.
A swift end to the war that favours the US might bolster Rubio, who also serves as Trump's national security adviser and could be seen as a steady hand during a crisis.
A prolonged conflict could give Vance space to argue he reflected the anti-war instincts of Trump's base without openly breaking with the president.
Trump's own standing is also at stake. His approval rating fell in recent days to 36 per cent, its lowest point since he returned to the White House, hit by a surge in fuel prices and widespread disapproval of the Iran war, a four-day Reuters/Ipsos poll completed last week found.
Some Republicans say they are watching closely for which senior aide Trump appears to favour as the Iran conflict unfolds. Some see signs of Trump leaning towards Rubio but note he could change his mind quickly.
"Everyone is watching the body language that Trump makes on Rubio and not seeing the same on Vance," a Republican with close ties to the White House said.
The White House rejected the idea that Trump is signalling a preference.
"No amount of crazed media speculation about Vice-President Vance and Secretary Rubio will deter this administration's mission of fighting for the American people," spokesman Steven Cheung said.
Vance, 41, a former Marine who served in Iraq, has long argued against US entanglements in foreign wars.
His public comments on Iran have been limited and calibrated, and Trump has noted the two have "philosophical differences" on the conflict.
Once a self-described "never-Trumper", Vance wrote an opinion piece in the
Wall Street Journal
in 2023 saying Trump's best foreign ​policy was not starting any wars during his first four ​years in office between 2017 and 2021.
Standing alongside Trump in the Oval Office earlier this month, Vance said he supported Trump's handling of the war and agreed with him that Iran should not obtain a nuclear weapon.
Vance could take on a more direct role in negotiations if Trump's special envoy Steve Witkoff and son-in-law Jared Kushner make sufficient progress, a person with knowledge of the matter said.
A senior White House official said, Trump tolerates ideological differences as long as aides remain loyal, adding that Vance's sceptical views have helped inform Trump about where part of his voter base stands.
A person familiar with Vance's views told Reuters the vice-president will wait until after the November mid-term elections before deciding on whether to run in 2028.
Rubio, 54, has said he will not run for president if Vance does, and sources familiar with Rubio's views say he would be content as Vance's running mate.
But any perceived vulnerability for Vance could encourage Rubio and other Republicans eyeing bids.
"Trump has a long memory," said Republican strategist Ron Bonjean.
"And he may call out Vance for his lack of allegiance. And if Trump remains popular with the MAGA base, that could hurt him by not getting the endorsement of the president."
The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Include us in RM1,000 tourism tax relief to boost domestic tourism, Malaysia’s budget hotels tell govt
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 31 — The Malaysian government should encourage more Malaysians to travel locally by giving them tax relief for hotel stays, an industry group has suggested.
Malaysia Budget & Business Hotel Association (MyBHA) president Sri Ganesh Michiel said this proposed tax measure would help with the expected reduced number of overseas visitors due to the Middle East conflict.
“As global travel costs rise and geopolitical instability in West Asia/Middle East disrupts traditional overseas routes, we strongly urge the government to pivot towards domestic resilience by adding and expanding the RM1,000 tourism tax relief to include licensed and registered accommodation,” he told
Malay Mail
.
“We must proactively incentivise Malaysians to choose local destinations to balance the inevitable shortfall in international arrivals,” he said.
But he said this tax relief must be strictly only for regulated hotels to safeguard industry integrity and to ensure it supports businesses that contribute to Malaysia’s national economy.
“By making ‘Cuti-Cuti Malaysia’ a more financially attractive option than increasingly expensive and volatile foreign travel, we can insulate our tourism sector from external shocks and ensure the absolute success of Visit Malaysia 2026,” said Sri Ganesh, who is also Malaysian Tourism Federation president.
